input.actions_taken: Confirmed via docker logs that sreft-web was on RELEASE=v2 and intentionally failing /orders. Compared env with payments (RELEASE=v1, healthy). Recreated the web container on the same network (sreft-bad-deploy_default) with network alias "web", port 8083:8080, and RELEASE=v1 (MODE=serve, PORT=8080). Verified /orders returns 200 {"ok":true,"release":"v1"}.
input.postmortem:
A bad release (v2) of the web/deploy-svc was rolled out without catching a regression that broke readiness and the /orders checkout path. The process stayed up, so "container running" health alone was insufficient; application-level readiness and canary checks would have blocked or limited blast radius. Rollback to the last known-good release (v1) restored service quickly with minimal risk.

What went well: clear error logging named the bad release and the previous good one, enabling a fast diagnosis.

Improvements: (1) gate deploys on readiness and synthetic checkout probes; (2) progressive delivery / canary with automatic rollback on error-rate or readiness failure; (3) keep RELEASE (or image tag) as a first-class, easily rolled-back config; (4) avoid relying solely on process liveness for production health. No blame—this is a classic missing progressive-delivery / readiness-gate gap.
input.root_cause: The web service was deployed with RELEASE=v2, which contains a regression that fails readiness checks and returns HTTP 500 on /orders (checkout path). Logs explicitly stated: "RELEASE=v2 failing readiness and returning 500s on /orders (regression introduced in this release; previous good release was v1)". The container was running but serving the broken release; payments/DB were healthy.
